title: Why Do Auctions Make People Pay Too Much?

challenges.stem: Your company is one of 15 firms bidding on a government infrastructure contract. You estimate the project will cost $8M to execute. You want to win. What does the winner's curse tell you about how to bid?

options.text: Bid above $8M (include profit margin) but NOT aggressively below — because with 15 bidders, whoever estimates lowest probably underestimated costs, and winning means you're likely the one who got the math wrong
Bid at exactly $8M (break-even) to maximize your chance of winning
Bid well below $8M to ensure you beat all 14 competitors
Bid 20% above your estimate to account for unknown risks

challenges.explanation: The winner's curse in procurement: with 15 bidders, the 'winner' (lowest bidder in a contract auction) is the one who underestimated costs the most. Bidding at break-even or below maximizes winning probability but guarantees losses when reality exceeds your estimate. You should bid ABOVE your estimate (option D is close but 'unknown risks' isn't the same as 'winner's curse adjustment'). The correct framing: discount your optimism about costs, don't amplify it.

challenges.stem: At a charity auction, a person who planned to spend $100 ends up paying $500 for a bottle of wine worth $40. The room was electric with bidding energy. What force overrode their pre-set budget?

options.text: Scarcity — competitive arousal triggered by visible rivals + incremental 'just $10 more' framing + social proof that others want it = emotional override of rational budget
Status — they wanted to appear generous in front of the crowd
Incentives — the charity cause made them feel the money was going somewhere good regardless of the wine value
Identity — they see themselves as someone who wins and couldn't accept losing publicly

challenges.explanation: While status (B), charity motivation (C), and competitive identity (D) may contribute, the core mechanism is scarcity-driven competitive arousal: rivals bidding creates urgency (scarcity of the item + scarcity of 'winning opportunity'), each increment feels small ('just $10 more'), and others' bids validate value (social proof). The auction STRUCTURE exploits these biases — not the individual's character flaws.

pairs.back: In competitive bidding for uncertain-value items, the winner is the person who overestimated the most. Winning IS evidence of overpaying.
Highest bidder wins but pays second-highest bid. Optimal strategy: always bid your true value. Produces honest revelation through mechanism design.
Being outbid activates the same brain circuits as personal insult. The bid becomes about WINNING, not value. This is why auction houses serve champagne.
The same rational bidders produce wildly different outcomes under different rules. If you didn't design the auction, you're probably being played by it.

pairs.right: Maximizes competitive escalation and winner's curse — great for sellers, bad for buyers
Incentivizes honest bidding since you pay second price — reduces overpayment

pairs.left: Ken Binmore's 3G spectrum design (5 licenses, 4 incumbents)
pairs.right: Guaranteed new-entrant competition — prevented collusion among existing players

pairs.left: 'Just $50 more' thinking across 10 rounds of bidding
pairs.right: Incremental framing that disguises total overpayment — anchor to total, not increment

pairs.left: Setting your maximum before entering the auction room
pairs.right: Pre-commitment device against in-the-moment competitive arousal override

before.label: Playing to win
before.text: I know what this is worth and I'm going to win it. If others are bidding this high, it validates my estimate. Just one more bid and I'll have it.

after.label: Playing to not lose
after.text: I set my maximum before entering. If winning requires exceeding it, I stop — because winning an auction is statistical evidence I'm the biggest overestimator in the room. I ask who designed these rules, and in whose interest.
